text: Okay, today I'll show the abdominal procedure of a robotic esophagectomy. This patient has a T-two, N-zero, M-zero esophageal cancer in the middle throat, and so today I'll do the So this video is for the trainee and beginners for the educational purpose. I always use a three arm and in the left hand I like to use a med-land and in the number four arm I like to use a forced bipolar process. Usually this forcep, this arm is used for the holding or the pushing something. And for the dissection, usually mainly I like to use the harmonic, but sometimes I use a scissor or something else.

The surgeon begins the procedure by dividing the lesser omentum, revealing the caudate lobe of the liver. As the omental tissue is divided upwards, the right crus of the diaphragm becomes visible. The area around the crus is cleared in preparation for placing a holding suture.

The surgeon places a traction suture over the xiphoid process using a straight long needle, suturing it to the crux of the diaphragm. The suture is passed underneath the costal margin, enabling retraction of the liver upwards.
